verses.text: The men of Ephraim said unto him, Why hast thou served us thus, that thou calledst us not, when thou wentest to fight with the Midianites? And they did chide with him sharply. He said unto them, What have I done now in comparison of you? Is not the gleaning of the grapes of Ephraim better than the vintage of Abi-ezer? God hath delivered into your hands the princes of Midian, Oreb and Zeeb: and what was I able to do in comparison of you? Then their anger was abated toward him, when he had said that. Guidance: Ephraim is touchy about prestige. They were not called for the original 300 but did join the river-pursuit. They complain not about the fight but the slight. Gideon answers with a soft proverb: 'the gleaning of Ephraim is better than the vintage of Abi-ezer (my own clan)' — i.e., what you cleaned up at the end was greater than what I started with. A leader gives away the credit and keeps the peace. Proverbs 15:1 in action: a soft answer turns away wrath.

Gideon came to Jordan, and passed over, he, and the three hundred men that were with him, faint, yet pursuing them. He said unto the men of Succoth, Give, I pray you, loaves of bread unto the people that follow me; for they be faint, and I am pursuing after Zebah and Zalmunna, kings of Midian. Guidance: 'Faint, yet pursuing them' — five words that name what real obedience often looks like. Tired and not quitting. The 300 cross the Jordan into east-bank territory and ask Israelite kinsmen (Succoth, a Gadite town) for bread. A simple request from soldiers fighting their war on their behalf. The next verse will show what happens when fellow believers refuse to feed each other in the fight.

He said unto the men of Succoth, Give, I pray you, loaves of bread unto the people that follow me; for they be faint, and I am pursuing after Zebah and Zalmunna, kings of Midian. Guidance: 'Faint, yet pursuing them' — five words that name what real obedience often looks like. Tired and not quitting. The 300 cross the Jordan into east-bank territory and ask Israelite kinsmen (Succoth, a Gadite town) for bread. A simple request from soldiers fighting their war on their behalf. The next verse will show what happens when fellow believers refuse to feed each other in the fight.

The princes of Succoth said, Are the hands of Zebah and Zalmunna now in thine hand, that we should give bread unto thine army? Gideon said, Therefore when the LORD hath delivered Zebah and Zalmunna into mine hand, then I will tear your flesh with the thorns of the wilderness and with briers. Guidance: A cynical answer: 'show me the heads first, and then we'll feed you.' Succoth's leaders make survival their first commitment. They have heard of Gideon's victory but want certainty before risking anything. Gideon's reply is hard: thorns. He is not joking. The vow will be kept (v16). Failing fellow Israelites in their hour of need has a cost. The east-bank tribes will pay for assuming the war was always somebody else's.

He went up thence to Penuel, and spake unto them likewise: and the men of Penuel answered him as the men of Succoth had answered him. He spake also unto the men of Penuel, saying, When I come again in peace, I will break down this tower. Guidance: Penuel — where Jacob wrestled God and was renamed Israel (Gen 32) — refuses the same way. The town named for a face-to-face encounter with God now turns its face away from God's deliverer. Their tower is their pride. Gideon's reply: 'I will break it down.' The most fortified structure in town will be the first wreckage when he returns.

He went up thence to Penuel, and spake unto them likewise: and the men of Penuel answered him as the men of Succoth had answered him. He spake also unto the men of Penuel, saying, When I come again in peace, I will break down this tower. Guidance: Penuel — where Jacob wrestled God and was renamed Israel (Gen 32) — refuses the same way. The town named for a face-to-face encounter with God now turns its face away from God's deliverer. Their tower is their pride. Gideon's reply: 'I will break it down.' The most fortified structure in town will be the first wreckage when he returns.

Zebah and Zalmunna were in Karkor, and their hosts with them, about fifteen thousand men, all that were left of all the hosts of the children of the east: for there fell an hundred and twenty thousand men that drew sword. Gideon went up by the way of them that dwelt in tents on the east of Nobah and Jogbehah, and smote the host: for the host was secure. When Zebah and Zalmunna fled, he pursued after them, and took the two kings of Midian, Zebah and Zalmunna, and discomfited all the host. Guidance: The original camp held 135,000 men; 120,000 fell that first night. Of the 15,000 remnant, the two kings ('sacrifice' and 'protection denied' — name puns the Hebrew exploits) sit in Karkor thinking themselves safe. Gideon comes by the nomad route (the back trail no army would expect) and surprises them. The kings flee; Gideon catches them. 'Faint yet pursuing' bears its fruit. The book has just made the math: 300 of God's men finished what 32,000 could not have.

Gideon son of Joash returned from battle before the sun was up. He caught a young man of the men of Succoth, and enquired of him: and he described unto him the princes of Succoth, and the elders thereof, even threescore and seventeen men. He came unto the men of Succoth, and said, Behold Zebah and Zalmunna, with whom ye did upbraid me, saying, Are the hands of Zebah and Zalmunna now in thine hand, that we should give bread unto thy men that are weary? He took the elders of the city, and thorns of the wilderness and briers, and with them he taught the men of Succoth. Guidance: A young man of Succoth provides the city's leadership roster — 77 names. Gideon does not act on rumor; he uses real records. Then he stages the lesson: 'Behold Zebah and Zalmunna' — the very kings Succoth said they wanted to see before believing. He fulfills his vow exactly as he spoke it. The verb 'taught' is deliberate; the Hebrew word for lessons. Sometimes the truth needs more than words. The east-bank kinsmen learn that supporting God's people in the fight is not optional charity.

He beat down the tower of Penuel, and slew the men of the city. Guidance: The second vow is kept too. The tower that was Penuel's pride is rubble. The men of the city die — apparently they had been the chief naysayers. The chapter does not soften Gideon. He keeps two harsh promises in two verses. Whether the reader approves of his methods or not, the narrator wants the verb visible: 'beat down.' What rises against the LORD's deliverer falls.

Then said he unto Zebah and Zalmunna, What manner of men were they whom ye slew at Tabor? They answered, As thou art, so were they; each one resembled the children of a king. He said, They were my brethren, even the sons of my mother: as the LORD liveth, if ye had saved them alive, I would not slay you. He said unto Jether his firstborn, Up, and slay them. But the youth drew not his sword: for he feared, because he was yet a youth. Then Zebah and Zalmunna said, Rise thou, and fall upon us: for as the man is, so is his strength. And Gideon arose, and slew Zebah and Zalmunna, and took away the ornaments that were on their camels' necks. Guidance: A previously untold detail — Midian had killed Gideon's own brothers at Tabor. The victory has a personal undercurrent. Gideon offers his firstborn the honor of execution, perhaps to confer maturity. The boy is too young, too afraid. The kings themselves, with a kind of dignity, demand a man's hand to end them. Gideon obliges. The chapter that began with magnanimity (v1-3) ends with personal vengeance — the same hand that took the bread from Succoth's children also takes the lives of the kings who took his brothers.

Then the men of Israel said unto Gideon, Rule thou over us, both thou, and thy son, and thy son's son also: for thou hast delivered us from the hand of Midian. Gideon said unto them, I will not rule over you, neither shall my son rule over you: the LORD shall rule over you. Guidance: Israel asks for a hereditary monarchy 250 years before they will ask Samuel for one. Gideon's answer is theologically perfect: 'The LORD shall rule over you.' He understands what the people don't — that asking for a king is asking to be governed by less than they already have. The verse is a high-water mark of Gideon's faith. The next paragraph will show how quickly he undoes it.

The next paragraph will show how quickly he undoes it.

Gideon said unto them, I would desire a request of you, that ye would give me every man the earrings of his prey. (For they had golden earrings, because they were Ishmaelites.) They answered, We willingly give them. They spread a garment, and did cast therein every man the earrings of his prey. The weight of the golden earrings that he requested was a thousand and seven hundred shekels of gold; beside ornaments, and collars, and purple raiment that was on the kings of Midian, and beside the chains that were about their camels' necks. Gideon made an ephod thereof, and put it in his city, even in Ophrah: and all Israel went thither a whoring after it: which thing became a snare unto Gideon, and to his house. Guidance: The same man who refused a crown asks for the gold. Forty pounds of it. He makes an ephod — a priestly vestment — and installs it at Ophrah, his hometown. The narrator does not bother explaining his motive; he just records the fallout: 'all Israel went thither a whoring after it.' The man who tore down a Baal altar in chapter 6 builds a different kind of shrine in chapter 8. Even faithful leaders fail at the place of their greatest success. The chapter calls the ephod what it is: 'a snare unto Gideon and to his house.' His seventy sons will die because of what he built here.

Thus was Midian subdued before the children of Israel, so that they lifted up their heads no more. And the country was in quietness forty years in the days of Gideon. Guidance: 'In the days of Gideon' — a peace pegged to a man's lifetime, not to the people's faithfulness. Forty years. The same number as Othniel's tenure. But the difference now: an ephod sits in Ophrah, and the worship of the LORD has a competitor next door. Quietness is not the same as covenant fidelity; surface calm can hide a slow infection.

Jerubbaal son of Joash went and dwelt in his own house. Gideon had threescore and ten sons of his body begotten: for he had many wives. His concubine that was in Shechem, she also bare him a son, whose name he called Abimelech. Gideon son of Joash died in a good old age, and was buried in the sepulchre of Joash his father, in Ophrah of the Abi-ezrites. Guidance: Seventy sons, many wives, and a concubine in Shechem. The man who refused a crown built a king's harem. Abimelech ('my father is king') is named with bitter irony — Gideon refused the throne but called his Shechemite son 'my-father-king.' Names betray heart positions. Chapter 9 will reveal what happens when an Abimelech with that name and a city-base in Shechem decides to take seriously what his father only pretended to refuse. Even good deaths have unfinished business in the next room.

As soon as Gideon was dead, the children of Israel turned again, and went a whoring after Baalim, and made Baal-berith their god. And the children of Israel remembered not the LORD their God, who had delivered them out of the hands of all their enemies on every side: neither shewed they kindness to the house of Jerubbaal, namely, Gideon, according to all the goodness which he had shewed unto Israel. Guidance: The book's recurring tragedy, one more turn. 'As soon as Gideon was dead' — the burial dust had not yet settled. 'Baal-berith' means 'Baal-of-the-covenant' — Israel literally invents a covenant rival to the LORD's covenant. And then the second indictment: 'neither shewed they kindness to the house of Jerubbaal.' Ingratitude not just to God but to the man who saved them. Chapter 9 will show the murder of his seventy sons as the consequence. The chapter that opened with Ephraim's complaint about not being honored ends with all Israel failing to honor the man who saved them. The Spirit's old refrain: choose this day whom ye will serve. Israel keeps not.

As soon as Gideon was dead, the children of Israel turned again, and went a whoring after Baalim, and made Baal-berith their god. And the children of Israel remembered not the LORD their God, who had delivered them out of the hands of all their enemies on every side: neither shewed they kindness to the house of Jerubbaal, namely, Gideon, according to all the goodness which he had shewed unto Israel. Guidance: The book's recurring tragedy, one more turn. 'As soon as Gideon was dead' — the burial dust had not yet settled. 'Baal-berith' means 'Baal-of-the-covenant' — Israel literally invents a covenant rival to the LORD's covenant. And then the second indictment: 'neither shewed they kindness to the house of Jerubbaal.' Ingratitude not just to God but to the man who saved them. Chapter 9 will show the murder of his seventy sons as the consequence. The chapter that opened with Ephraim's complaint about not being honored ends with all Israel failing to honor the man who saved them. The Spirit's old refrain: choose this day whom ye will serve. Israel keeps not.
